sentence: If it ______ tomorrow, we will stay at home.
explanation: First conditional: <If> + present simple, <will> + base verb. Used for real/possible future situations.
tip: Think about the weather forecast — what verb form goes after 'if'?

sentence: If you study hard, you ______ the exam.
explanation: First conditional: the result clause uses <will> + base verb for likely outcomes.
tip: What's the likely result of studying hard?

sentence: If I ______ a million dollars, I would travel the world.
explanation: Second conditional: <If> + past simple, <would> + base verb. Used for unreal/hypothetical present situations.
tip: This is imaginary — what past form expresses an unreal situation?

sentence: If she spoke French, she ______ get that job in Paris.
explanation: Second conditional: the result clause uses <would> + base verb for hypothetical outcomes.
tip: She doesn't speak French — what auxiliary shows an imaginary result?

sentence: We will go to the beach if the weather ______ nice.
explanation: First conditional: the <if> clause uses present simple, even though we're talking about the future.
tip: After 'if' in first conditional, use present simple — not 'will'.

sentence: If I ______ you, I would apologize immediately.
explanation: Second conditional: we use <were> for all persons with <be> in hypothetical situations (not <was>).
tip: In formal/hypothetical English, what form of 'be' do we use after 'if I'?

sentence: If they arrive late, they ______ miss the beginning of the film.
explanation: First conditional: <will> + base verb in the result clause for a real possibility.
tip: Arriving late is possible — what auxiliary shows a real future consequence?

sentence: If he ______ more money, he would buy a bigger house.
explanation: Second conditional: past simple in the <if> clause for an unreal present situation.
tip: He doesn't have enough money — what past form shows this isn't real?

sentence: I will call you if I ______ any news.
explanation: First conditional: present simple after <if> for a possible future event.
tip: Getting news is possible — use present simple after 'if'.

sentence: If we ______ closer to the office, we wouldn't need a car.
explanation: Second conditional: past simple for an unreal present situation.
tip: We don't live close — what past form shows this hypothetical situation?

sentence: She will be angry if you ______ tell her the truth.
explanation: First conditional: negative present simple in the <if> clause.
tip: Not telling the truth is a real possibility — negate the present simple.

sentence: If I could fly, I ______ visit every country in the world.
explanation: Second conditional: <would> + base verb for an impossible/imaginary result.
tip: Flying is impossible for humans — what auxiliary shows the imaginary result?

sentence: If the bus ______ on time, we won't be late for school.
explanation: First conditional: present simple in the <if> clause for a real possibility.
tip: The bus might come on time — use present simple.

sentence: If I ______ the president, I would improve public education.
explanation: Second conditional: <were> (not <was>) for hypothetical situations with <be>.
tip: Being president is hypothetical — use the subjunctive form of 'be'.

sentence: You will feel better if you ______ some rest.
explanation: First conditional: present simple after <if> for advice about a likely outcome.
tip: Getting rest is a real action you can take — present simple after 'if'.

sentence: If we ______ a garden, we would grow our own vegetables.
explanation: Second conditional: past simple for something we don't have but wish we did.
tip: We don't have a garden — what past form shows this unreal situation?

sentence: If you ______ that button, the machine will start.
explanation: First conditional: present simple for a real cause-and-effect relationship.
tip: Pressing the button is a real action — use present simple.

sentence: If she ______ harder, she would get better grades.
explanation: Second conditional: past simple for advice about an unreal present situation.
tip: She doesn't study hard enough — what past form shows this hypothetical change?

sentence: I won't go to the party if nobody ______ me.
explanation: First conditional: present simple in the <if> clause, even in negative result clauses.
tip: Being invited is a real possibility — present simple after 'if'.

sentence: If I ______ speak Chinese, I would move to Beijing.
explanation: Second conditional: <could> (past of <can>) in the <if> clause for hypothetical ability.
tip: You can't speak Chinese — what past modal shows imaginary ability?

sentence: If the weather improves, we ______ go hiking this weekend.
explanation: First conditional: <will> in the result clause for a real future plan.
tip: The weather might improve — what auxiliary shows the real future result?

sentence: If I ______ taller, I would play basketball professionally.
explanation: Second conditional: <were> for hypothetical physical characteristics.
tip: You can't change your height — use the hypothetical form of 'be'.

sentence: We will save money if we ______ cooking at home.
explanation: First conditional: present simple for a realistic action with a real benefit.
tip: Starting to cook at home is a real possibility — present simple.

sentence: If they ______ us, we would help them move to their new flat.
explanation: Second conditional: past simple for a situation that hasn't happened (they haven't asked).
tip: They haven't asked — what past form shows this unreal condition?

sentence: If you don't hurry, you ______ miss your flight.
explanation: First conditional: <will> for a real warning about a likely consequence.
tip: Missing the flight is a real risk — what auxiliary shows this warning?
